184	namespace Modules
185	
186	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
187	
188	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
189	
190	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
191	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
192	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
193	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
194	
195	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
196	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
197	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
198	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
199	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
200	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
201	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
202	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
203	
204	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
205	
206	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
207	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
208	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
209	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
210	
211	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
212	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
213	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
214	sheafification. -/
215	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
216	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
217	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
218	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
219	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
220	
221	/-! ## §2. (Off the critical path) the full monoidal-category structure
222	
223	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
224	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
225	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
226	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal
227	coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and no `MonoidalClosed` structure is ever
228	consumed. The earlier `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance (and the two
229	localization-transport supplements `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
230	`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) routed through the verified-absent
231	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall; they are removed here. The group
232	law is built directly on the line-bundle subcategory from the four
233	existence-of-iso lemmas below, mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`. -/
234	
235	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
236	
237	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
238	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
239	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
240	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
241	
242	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
243	
244	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
245	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
246	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
247	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
248	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
249	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
250	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
251	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
252	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
253	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
254	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
255	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
256	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
257	
258	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
259	sheaf.**
260	
261	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
262	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
263	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
264	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
265	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
266	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
267	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
268	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
269	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
270	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
271	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
272	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
273	
274	/-- **Refining a trivialisation to a smaller open.** If `M` is trivialised on an
275	open `U` (`M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U`), it is trivialised on every open `W ≤ U`.
276	
277	The chart-chase is identical in spirit to `LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`:
278	factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`
279	and `restrictFunctorComp` to identify `M.restrict W.ι` with
280	`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
281	`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
282	via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
283	because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
284	noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
285	    (hWU : W ≤ U)
286	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
287	    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
288	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
289	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
290	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]
291	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
292	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
293	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
294	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
295	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
296	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
297	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
298	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
299	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
300	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso
301	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M) ≪≫ ?_
302	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj 𝒪_U
303	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso e ≪≫ ?_
304	  -- ≅ 𝒪_W
305	  haveI hI : IsIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit j.toRingCatSheafHom) := inferInstance
306	  exact @asIso _ _ _ _ _ hI
307	
308	/-- **Substrate tensor commutes with restriction along an open immersion.**
309	
310	For an open immersion `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M N : X.Modules`,
311	`(tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f)`.
312	
313	This is the single missing-infrastructure ingredient of `A.1.c.SubT`. It says
314	the substrate `⊗` (sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor) commutes
315	with the restriction functor `restrictFunctor f ≅ pullback f` along an open
316	immersion. Mathematically it factors as: (i) the presheaf-of-modules tensor
317	commutes with the pullback of presheaves (sectionwise this is the
318	extension-of-scalars identity
319	`B ⊗_A (P ⊗_A Q) ≅ (B ⊗_A P) ⊗_B (B ⊗_A Q)` for `A → B`, Stacks 03DM); and
320	(ii) sheafification commutes with pullback along an open immersion (the
321	restriction is exact and the small-Zariski sheafification is local). Neither is
322	in Mathlib at the `SheafOfModules` level (there is no monoidal structure on
323	`SheafOfModules`, hence no strong-monoidal pullback). A genuine construction
324	requires either the `Localization.Monoidal` transport of the monoidal structure
325	through `PresheafOfModules.sheafification.IsLocalization` together with
326	`W.IsMonoidal` for `W := (J.W).inverseImage (toPresheaf _)` (an instance NOT
327	present in Mathlib and the real residual obstacle), or a direct sectionwise
328	construction. Left as a named typed `sorry` feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`;
329	see the task result for the precise missing instance statement. -/
330	noncomputable def tensorObj_restrict_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
331	    [IsOpenImmersion f] (M N : X.Modules) :
332	    (tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f) := by
333	  -- Step 1. Reduce `restrict` to `pullback` along the open immersion `f`
334	  -- (`restrictFunctorIsoPullback`, Mathlib).
335	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback f).app (tensorObj M N) ≪≫ ?_
336	  -- Step 2. **Sheafification commutes with pullback.** `tensorObj M N` is, by
337	  -- definition, `sheafification.obj (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj
338	  -- M.val N.val)`, so the genuine Mathlib lemma
339	  -- `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`
340	  -- (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackContinuous`,
341	  -- `sheafification ⋙ pullback φ ≅ PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom ⋙
342	  -- sheafification`) moves the pullback *inside* the sheafification. This
343	  -- discharges half (ii) of the original obstruction (sheafification commuting
344	  -- with pullback). After it the goal is the purely **presheaf-level** residual
345	  -- `sheafify ((PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val))
346	  --    ≅ (M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)`.
347	  refine (SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback f.toRingCatSheafHom).app
348	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val) ≪≫ ?_
349	  -- Step 3. **Strip the outer sheafification.** Both sides are
350	  -- `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.obj)` applied to a
351	  -- presheaf-of-modules: the LHS to `(pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)`, and
352	  -- the RHS `(M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)` *by definition* to
353	  -- `(M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`. So it suffices to give the
354	  -- comparison at the PRESHEAF level and sheafify it. This is a genuine reduction
355	  -- step (verified: the goal below has no sheafification).
356	  refine (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.obj)).mapIso ?_
357	  -- Step 4 (RESIDUAL — CORRECTED iter-208 analysis). The remaining presheaf goal is
358	  --   `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)
359	  --      ≅ (M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`
360	  -- where `φ.hom = (Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom f).hom` and `⊗ₚ =
361	  -- PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`. The Route-A recipe's claim that this is
362	  -- a "~30–60 LOC sectionwise unfolding of `pullback`" is INCORRECT:
363	  -- `PresheafOfModules.pullback` is the OPAQUE abstract left adjoint
364	  -- `(pushforward φ.hom).leftAdjoint` (`Presheaf/Pullback.lean:44`) — it has NO
365	  -- sectionwise formula, and the kaehler precedent (`analogies/...presheafpullback`
366	  -- Decision 5) EXCISED the analogous unfolding. The recipe conflates the opaque
367	  -- `pullback` with the CONCRETE `restrict`/`pushforward` (`restrict_obj` is `rfl`
368	  -- because `M.restrict f = restrictFunctor f = SheafOfModules.pushforward β`, NOT
369	  -- a pullback).
370	  --
371	  -- The genuine route needs TWO project-side ingredients, BOTH absent from Mathlib:
372	  --  (H1, the linchpin) a PRESHEAF-level iso `pushforward β.hom ≅ pullback φ.hom`,
373	  --      where `β.hom` is the open-immersion structure map of `restrictFunctor f`
374	  --      (so `(M.restrict f).val = (pushforward β.hom).obj M.val` definitionally).
375	  --      Obtain it via `Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq` from a presheaf-level
376	  --      `pushforward β.hom ⊣ pushforward φ.hom` — the presheaf analogue of the
377	  --      sheaf-level `SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj`
378	  --      (`Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:226`). That analogue needs presheaf-level
379	  --      `pushforwardNatTrans` and `pushforwardCongr` (only the SHEAF versions exist).
380	  --  (H2) the strong-monoidal comparison `(pushforward β.hom).obj (A ⊗ₚ B) ≅
381	  --      (pushforward β.hom).obj A ⊗ₚ (pushforward β.hom).obj B`. Since
382	  --      `pushforward = pushforward₀ ⋙ restrictScalars` (`Presheaf/Pushforward.lean:86`),
383	  --      `pushforward₀OfCommRingCat` is already `Monoidal`, and `β` is along the
384	  --      ring ISO `f.appIso`, this reduces to upgrading the file's lax
385	  --      `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` to STRONG via `Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal`
386	  --      (`Monoidal/Functor.lean:718`) — needing `IsIso ε` (easy: `ε` is the ring
387	  --      map `f.appIso`, iso) and `IsIso μ`. The `μ`-iso bottoms out in the absent
388	  --      Mathlib lemma "`ModuleCat.restrictScalars` along a ring iso is strong
389	  --      monoidal" (`extendScalars` is `Monoidal` but `restrictScalars` is only
390	  --      `LaxMonoidal`, `Monoidal/Adjunction.lean`).
391	  -- Closure once both land:
392	  --   `(pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)`
393	  --      `≅[H1.symm] (pushforward β.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)`
394	  --      `≅[H2] (pushforward β.hom M.val) ⊗ₚ (pushforward β.hom N.val)`
395	  --      `=defeq (M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`.
396	  -- This is ~200–300 LOC across 4 absent ingredients — `mathlib-build` scale, NOT a
397	  -- single prove-mode close. See `informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md` for the full
398	  -- corrected decomposition with exact statements.
399	  sorry
400	
401	/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**
402	
403	If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
404	their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
405	`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. The proof picks, for each point `x`,
406	a common affine open `W ∋ x` contained in trivialising opens `U` (for `M`) and
407	`U'` (for `N`), refines both trivialisations to `W` via `restrictIsoUnitOfLE`,
408	then transports through `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, the bifunctoriality
409	`tensorObjIsoOfIso`, and the unit isomorphism `tensorObj_unit_iso`:
410	`(M ⊗ N)|_W ≅ M|_W ⊗ N|_W ≅ 𝒪_W ⊗ 𝒪_W ≅ 𝒪_W`. The only residual gap is the
411	substrate-restriction compatibility `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. -/
412	lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
413	    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
414	    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) := by
415	  intro x
416	  obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU_aff, ⟨eM⟩⟩ := hM x
417	  obtain ⟨U', hxU', hU'_aff, ⟨eN⟩⟩ := hN x
418	  obtain ⟨W, hW_aff, hxW, hWsub⟩ :=
419	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := X) (x := x) (U := U ⊓ U') ⟨hxU, hxU'⟩
420	  have hWU : W ≤ U := le_trans hWsub inf_le_left
421	  have hWU' : W ≤ U' := le_trans hWsub inf_le_right
422	  refine ⟨W, hxW, hW_aff, ⟨?_⟩⟩
423	  exact tensorObj_restrict_iso W.ι M N ≪≫
424	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU eM) (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU' eN) ≪≫
425	    tensorObj_unit_iso
426	
427	/-- **Inverse of an invertible module.**
428	
429	Every line bundle `L : X.Modules` has a two-sided tensor inverse: there is a
430	locally-trivial `Linv : X.Modules` (the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)`) together with
431	a tensor isomorphism `L ⊗_X Linv ≅ 𝒪_X`. Per blueprint
432	`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`. iter-206 flat-pivot: the designated unit is
433	`SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf = 𝒪_X` (the `MonoidalCategory` unit `𝟙_` is
434	no longer available — the full monoidal instance is off the critical path, see
435	§2). iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body builds the
436	dual and the contraction isomorphism, which is an isomorphism affine-locally on
437	a trivialising cover. -/
438	lemma exists_tensorObj_inverse {X : Scheme.{u}} {L : X.Modules}
439	    (hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L) :
440	    ∃ Linv : X.Modules, LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial Linv ∧
441	      Nonempty (tensorObj L Linv ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :=
442	  sorry
443	
444	/-- **Restriction of `⊗` to the `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier.**
445	
446	For `S`-schemes `C, T`, the bifunctor `⊗_{C ×_S T}` restricts to the subtype
447	`LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` of locally-trivial modules on `C ×_S T`, with unit

449	`lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`. -/

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
44	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
45	  \[
46	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
47	  \]
48	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
49	\end{definition}
50	\begin{lemma}
51	\leanok
52	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
53	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
54	  
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
56	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
57	  \[
58	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
59	  \]
60	\end{lemma}
61	\begin{lemma}
62	\leanok
63	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
64	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
65	  
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
68	\end{lemma}
69	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
74	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
78	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
79	  \[
80	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
81	  \]
82	  The natural strengthening would be to return
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
85	  \]
86	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
87	\end{remark}
88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
92	  \label{def:Jacobian}
93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}
98	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
99	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
100	\begin{theorem}\leanok
101	[Group object structure]
102	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
104	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
105	\end{theorem}
106	\begin{proof}
107	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
108	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
109	\end{proof}
110	\begin{theorem}\leanok
111	[Smoothness and dimension]
112	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
114	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-\(0\) smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced \(S\)); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
115	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:sm): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).
116	  % Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
117	  % \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\) Equality holds if and
118	  % only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
119	  % smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:ch0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).
121	  % Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is
122	  % of characteristic \(0\), then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension
123	  % \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
124	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:P0, smoothness clause): "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists,
125	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the
126	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\)
127	  % has an open group subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers
128	  % are the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then
129	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is smooth over \(S\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
131	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\).
132	\end{theorem}
133	\begin{proof}
134	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
135	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
136	\end{proof}
137	\begin{theorem}\leanok
138	[Properness]
139	  \label{thm:Jacobian_proper}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper}

142	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, and represents
143	  % \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are
144	  % smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) has an open group
145	  % subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers are the
146	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\)
147	  % is smooth over \(S\).  Moreover, if all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are complete
148	  % and if \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is separated over \(S\), then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is closed
149	  % in \(\IPic_{X/S}\) and proper over \(S\)."
150	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Prp.~(prp:P0).}

152	\end{theorem}
153	\begin{proof}
154	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{proper} : \mathrm{IsProper}\, J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness.
155	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which is itself proper over \(\Spec k\) because \(C\) is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for \(\Pic\) of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) (and hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) after translation) follows from properness of \(\Sym^g(C)\) together with the universal closedness of the surjection \(\sigma\). In either case, properness descends to \(\Jac(C)\).
156	\end{proof}
157	\begin{theorem}\leanok
158	[Geometric irreducibility]
159	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}
160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible}
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Lem.~lem:agps (the identity component \(G^0\) is open-closed, finite type, geometrically irreducible, commutes with field extension) + Prp.~prp:pic0 (specialisation to \(\IPicz_{X/k}\)); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~30, 31  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE (lem:agps): "Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme
163	  % locally of finite type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the
164	  % identity element \(e\).  \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.  \tu{(2)} Then \(G\)
165	  % is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open subscheme.  \tu{(3)}
166	  % Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it is
167	  % geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending \(k\)."
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:pic0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).
169	  % Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then
170	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically
171	  % reduced open subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the
172	  % identity \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
173	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
174	  % extending \(k\)."
175	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Lem.~(lem:agps), Prp.~(prp:pic0).}
176	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
177	\end{theorem}
178	\begin{proof}
179	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{geomIrred} : \mathrm{GeometricallyIrreducible}\, J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness.
180	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety \(\Jac(C)\) is the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, \(\Jac(C)\) arises as the Stein factorisation of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) followed by a translation; \(\Sym^g(C)\) is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of \(C^g\), itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
181	\end{proof}
182	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
183	\begin{itemize}
184	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
185	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
186	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
187	\end{itemize}
188	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
189	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
190	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
191	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
192	\begin{definition}
193	\leanok
194	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
195	[Albanese witness for a curve]
196	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
197	  
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
199	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) is a bundle of the following seven fields:
200	  \begin{itemize}
201	    \item \(J\): an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\) --- the candidate Albanese object.
202	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
203	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
204	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
205	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
206	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
207	    \item \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\): a proof that, for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
208	  \end{itemize}
209	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
210	\end{definition}
211	\begin{remark}
212	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
213	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
214	  \[
215	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
216	  \]
217	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single \(J\) is chosen once (intrinsic to \(C\)), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
218	  \[
219	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
220	  \]
221	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to \(C\): a different choice of \(P \in C(k)\) alters the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism of \(J\), and so leaves \(J\) unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-\(P\) projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), and \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) each take a marked point \(P\) as input and project the corresponding component of \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P\) via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
222	\end{remark}
223	\begin{remark}
224	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
225	  The fields \(\mathtt{smooth}\) and \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: \(\mathtt{smooth}\) is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}\) projection), whereas \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is the form required by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
226	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
227	\end{remark}

229	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
230	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
231

240	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Exercise ex:jac (generalized Jacobians of a family of curves form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\), projective iff \(X/S\) smooth); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers
242	  % are integral curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.
243	  % Show the ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
244	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this

246	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac).}
247	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
248	\end{theorem}
249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
270	  \end{itemize}
271	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
272	  \begin{itemize}
273	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
274	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
275	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
276	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
277	  \end{itemize}

1168	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1169	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1170	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1171	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1172	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1173	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1174	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1175	  \[
1176	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1177	  \]
1178	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1179	  abelian-group structure with addition
1180	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1181	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1182	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1183	  inverse
1184	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1185	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1186	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1187	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1188	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1189	  map
1190	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1191	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1192	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1193	\end{theorem}
1194	
1195	\begin{proof}
1196	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1197	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1198	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1199	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1200	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1201	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1202	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1203	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1204	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1205	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1206	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1207	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1208	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1209	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1210	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1211	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1212	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1213	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1214	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1215	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1216	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1217	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1218	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1219	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1220	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1221	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1222	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1223	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1224	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1225	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1226	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1227	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1228	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1229	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1230	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1231	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1232	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1233	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1234	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1235	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1236	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1237	
1238	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1239	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1240	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1241	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1242	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1243	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1244	
1245	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1246	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1247	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1248	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1249	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1250	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1251	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1252	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1253	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1254	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1255	\end{proof}
1256	
1257	\section{Out of scope}
1258	\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}
1259	
1260	The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
1261	sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
1262	\begin{itemize}
1263	  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
1264	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
1265	              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
1266	    \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} is established, the
1267	    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
1268	    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
1269	    against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}); none of those
1270	    declarations is the responsibility of this chapter.
1271	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
1272	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
1273	    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf;
1274	    representability is downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
1275	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
1276	    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
1277	    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials;

1279	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
1280	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
1281	    downstream of A.3.
1282	  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
1283	    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a
1284	    parallel Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical
1285	    path. Once the substrate ships in Mathlib, the
1286	    \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be slimmed to a single

1288	\end{itemize}
1289

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
